content:
# torch.compile RAUNE Didn't Work on RTX 3060 — Post-Mortem (2026-04-10)

chunzhe10/dorea#69 implemented and benchmarked but never shipped. Second dead-end
in the GPU optimization sweep after #68. Closing issue + parent sweep at this point.

## Measured results

| Config | Throughput | GPU busy | Visual YMAX |
|---|---|---|---|
| fp16 baseline (#67) | 4.32 fps | 227.7 ms | 0 |
| torch.compile reduce-overhead | 3.90 fps | 252.4 ms | 13 |

- Throughput: **-10%** (regression)
- GPU busy: **+11%** (regression)
- Visual diff: **YMAX 13/255 exceeds 5/255 tolerance**

## Root causes

### 1. RTX 3060 has too few SMs for Inductor's tile autotuning
Inductor explicitly logged: `Not enough SMs to use max_autotune_gemm mode`.
RTX 3060 has 28 SMs; Inductor's tile tuning is designed for A100-class
(108 SMs) and up. On consumer GPUs it generates kernels that don't match
cuDNN's already-tuned paths.

### 2. cuDNN eager mode is already optimal
RAUNE-Net is a straightforward U-Net with 30 residual blocks. cuDNN has
highly optimized implementations for this pattern on consumer Ampere.
There's no slack for Inductor to recover.

### 3. CUDA graph thread-locality deadlock
`mode="reduce-overhead"` captures CUDA graphs which are per-thread. When
the warmup forward pass runs on the main thread but the production batches
run from the GPU worker thread (3-thread pipeline), the graph capture is
bound to the wrong thread and `model(batch)` deadlocks. Removing the warmup
lets the worker thread capture on its first batch, but this negates the
main benefit of reduce-overhead mode.

### 4. Inductor fused kernels round fp16 slightly differently
The fused conv+bn+relu+add sequences produce different intermediate
precision than cuDNN's separate kernel calls. Worst-case diff hits 13/255
which is visible in isolated pixels, above the 5/255 tolerance threshold.

## Lesson: torch.compile isn't free on all hardware

Before proposing torch.compile as an optimization:
1. Check the target GPU's SM count against the model's resolution/batch
   characteristics. Under ~40 SMs, Inductor tile tuning is ineffective
2. Check if cuDNN already has optimized paths for the model's layer topology.
   U-Nets and ResNets on consumer Ampere are already well-tuned
3. If using reduce-overhead mode (CUDA graphs), verify graph capture happens
   on the SAME thread that will execute production inference — or use
   default mode (no CUDA graphs)
4. Benchmark with strict tolerance checks, not just throughput. Kernel
   fusion can change fp16 rounding order

## Pattern: GPU optimization sweep hit diminishing returns

Two issues closed wontfix in the sweep:
- #68 batched uploads (wrong premise: PCIe not on critical path)
- #69 torch.compile (wrong hardware: insufficient SMs, visual regression)

fp16 (#67) extracted the only real win. The 227 ms/frame of RAUNE compute
at fp16+cuDNN is essentially the hardware floor on RTX 3060 without
lifting quality constraints.

## Practical conclusion

**4.25 fps is the practical ceiling for direct mode on RTX 3060 + 6GB VRAM
under "no quality loss" constraint.** Further speedup requires:
- Lower proxy resolution (removed from scope per user constraint)
- Frame skipping (removed from scope per user constraint)
- Different model (separate architectural work)
- Upgraded hardware (not a software fix)

## What about NVDEC (issue #66 PR 3/3)?

Per-stage profile: decode is ~34 ms/frame, already hidden behind 230 ms GPU
in 3-thread pipeline. Eliminating decode entirely saves 0 wall-clock fps.
NVDEC would reduce CPU load but not throughput. Recommend closing #66 the
parent sweep as well.

## Related entries
- 019d7501: fp16 final decision (the win)
- 019d753f: upload batching post-mortem (first wontfix)
- This entry: torch.compile post-mortem (second wontfix)

## Artifacts
- Branch feat/69-torch-compile was never pushed; deleted locally
- Issue #69 closed as "not planned"
- Parent issue #66 recommendation: also close after documenting NVDEC decision
